title: Investment losers to avoid scrutiny as part of NRF review
full_text:
Labor’s $15bn National Reconstruction Fund is set to be put under the microscope, but the controversial decision to quarantine nearly half its cash for investments that target a return less than Canberra’s own borrowing costs will not be examined.
The Industry Department this week launched a review of the NRF, led by Swati Dave, a member of the Reserve Bank’s governance committee and former chief executive of Export Finance Australia.
Announced 19 months after the NRF’s first investment, the six-month review will consider the design and effectiveness of legislation underpinning the investment body, alongside its governance and accountability arrangements.
Excluded, however, is a controversial amendment to the NRF’s mandate that quarantined a combined $6bn of its funding for low-yield investments in green energy projects and firms exposed to critical supply chains.
While rendered “out of scope”, the change marked a significant shift in the design of the NRF, which was conceived by Labor in an effort to revive domestic manufacturing by offering concessional financing.
Initially, under its mandate, the fund was required to target a return rate across all its investments of 2-3 per cent above the five-year government bond rate, which sits at roughly 4.4 per cent and serves as a proxy for the government’s medium-term borrowing costs.
But after the NRF struggled to find green energy projects that met that threshold, Industry Minister Tim Ayres in April established a new $5bn net-zero stream of the NRF, which instead targets a return of 1 per cent below the five-year bond rate.
Similarly, $1bn of NRF funding has been repurposed to support businesses affected by the conflict in the Gulf, providing zero-interest loans for firms in “critical supply chains” such as fuel, plastics, fertilisers and agricultural chemical production. On these investments, the targeted return rate is zero.
In both cases, the government’s borrowing costs outstrip the NRF’s targeted rate of return, meaning while it may make a small profit, taxpayers will effectively wear a loss on $6bn worth of its investments.
A spokesman for Senator Ayres defended the approach, saying the parameters matched the timelines and requirements of the original legislation.
UNSW economics professor Richard Holden said excluding the mandate amendment seemed “a little bit suspicious”, and the NRF’s “strange” approach to selecting investments warranted scrutiny.
As well as excluding its investment mandate, the review also will not examine any changes to the NRF’s enabling legislation in the next six months.
Asked if further amendments would be made, Senator Ayres’ office did not directly answer the question. Similarly, the NRF’s investments – which now number 30 after it announced on Thursday it had poured $10m into Queensland-based robotics and computing company Emesent – are off the table.
